summary: Gastrocnemius crosses two joints (knee and ankle), while soleus crosses only the ankle joint.
Soleus crosses two joints while gastrocnemius crosses only the ankle joint.
Both gastrocnemius and soleus cross only the ankle joint.
Soleus contains large venous sinuses and is a common site for deep vein thrombosis (DVT).
Gastrocnemius is the primary site for DVT in the lower limb.
DVT does not occur in the calf muscles.
The calf muscle pump (peripheral heart) aids venous return by compressing deep veins during walking, pushing blood toward the heart.
Venous return from the leg depends entirely on gravity.
The calf muscle pump only works during running, not walking.
Flat foot (pes planus) involves loss of the medial longitudinal arch, while club foot (talipes equinovarus) involves plantarflexion, inversion, and adduction of the foot.
Flat foot and club foot are the same condition with different names.
Club foot involves dorsiflexion and eversion of the foot.
